content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5660          322 5660              322 5901           322 6441          322 6444

June 16, 1980


Honorable Alan Robbins
State Senate
State Capitol, Room 5080
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Senator Robbins:

     I am sorry to inform you that in the course of the nearly
two years since Mr. Rosenthal's letter to you, he has left the
Commission. As a result, I have been asked to respond to your
letter in his place.

     In general, your letter appears to meet the concerns set
forth by Mr. Rosenthal in his 1978 letter. I have only two
comments. First, the Motion Picture Council should be made
aware that the estimated value of the card must be based on the
maximum reasonable use, rather than the average use, of the card.
This was explained in Mr. Rosenthal's letter. Second, in the
particular circumstances presented by the use of these cards,
your suggestion for reporting is acceptable so long as each
recipient, on his statement of economic interests, identifies
the donor of the card as "the Motion Picture Association of
America, the Theatre Association of California, and participating
theatres," and includes a statement that the list of partici-
pating theatres is on file with the Fair Political Practices
Commission. Of course, this reporting scheme will work only
if the Motion Picture Council provides the Fair Political
Practices Commission with the required lists.

     Although you have not actually asked our advice on the
free screening of Darth Vader's return, I can confirm that this
constitutes an additional gift to the viewers. It is reportable
along with the card.

Senator Alan Robbins
June 16, 1980
Page Two


I certainly hope that this clarifies the nature of reporting
required for these cards. If you have any further questions,
please do not hesitate to call me.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Legal Division

BAM:nrw

REPLY TO:
ROOM 5050
STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 445-3121

CONSULTANT:
RICK ROLLINS

OFFICE MANAGER:
GLADYS KEITH

LEGISLATIVE SECRETARY:
AMY WILLIAMS

STAFF SECRETARY:
CAROL SWEET

COMMITTEES:
INSURANCE AND
FINANCIAL INSTITUTIONS
VICE CHAIRMAN

JUDICIARY

LOCAL GOVERNMENT

INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS

MOTION PICTURE DEVELOPMENT
COUNCIL, CHAIRMAN

JOINT COMMITTEE ON THE
STRUCTURE OF THE JUDICIARY

Senator

ALAN BOBBINS

Representing the
San Fernando Valley

April 30, 1980

Mr. Lee C. Rosenthal, Chief
Legal Division
State of California Fair Political
Practices Commission
1100 K Street Building
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Lee:

I am writing to bring you up to date on the status of the motion
picture ID cards discussed in your letter of August 7, 1978. It
has taken almost two years to work out the details, but after a
great deal of work we have done so and we anticipate issuing the
1981 theater identification cards just before the end of this
year.

Before we actually issued the cards we wanted to make certain that
we had ascertained a listing of policies of the various theater
chains in California (some will not honor passes on certain nights,
some honor passes with no charge, and some with a charge of sixty
or seventy-five cents per person). We need this information for
reporting purposes and also to avoid embarrassment for any legislator who uses the card. We were also delayed because the rather
complicated arrangements between theater owners and motion picture
production companies (percentage of gross contracts) may require
the cooperation of the production companies. From your previous
letter I have presumed that the Motion Picture Association of
America (the production company trade association) should be listed
for reporting purposes along with the Theatre Association of
California.

Pursuant to our telephone conversation following the letter of
August 7, 1978, I am presuming that the individual legislator can
disclose the Theatre Association of California and the Motion Picture
Association of America as donors and that the Motion Picture Council
will provide the FPPC with a list of cooperating theatre chains and
production companies (the list will be fairly long since some of
the cooperating companies will only release one or two pictures a
year). In stating this, I am presuming that it would be a needless
waste of copying expense to have each reporter attach a copy of
this lengthy list to his or her annual report. Our intent at this
time is to supply the legislators and you with an updated listing
of the qualifying owners and production companies every 90 days

Mr. Lee C. Rosenthal, Chief                    -2-                    April 30, 1980


since the policies of various individual companies will change
periodically and from picture to picture. By the way of explana-
tion, a production company may allow passes on most features, but
may decline to do so on a major high-budget film which would other-
wise be a sell-out at most performances. We will request all card
recipients to add a notation on their report form to "contact FPPC
or the Motion Picture Council for details."

On the initial year of the card (1981), the MPC will make an estimate
in advance of anticipated value based upon the factors discussed in
our prior correspondence. Tentatively this will be 15 estimated uses
of the card per year, at $3 per use per person, for two people (15x3x2=$90).
We will review this estimated valuation late in 1981 and at that time
we will have almost a year of experience to assist our Council in
establishing an estimated value for future years after 1981.

While we are on the subject of movies, let me take this opportunity
to tell you that Darth Vader escaped at the conclusion of Star Wars.
Since 20th Century Fox has done a sequel, we will be coordinating
a sequel to our previous highly successful Sacramento screening.
Since I presume that the applicable reporting rules are unchanged
and now that we are experienced at this, will it be necessary for
us to obtain a sequel to your previous well thought letter on
reporting consequences? If any problem developed on the screening
please let me know and we will dispatch ten studio extras armed
with laser guns to destroy the FPPC files on the subject.

By the way, my term as Chairman of the Motion Picture Council ends
this year and it appears that your father, Hersch Rosenthal, will
be taking over the very pleasant duty of coordinating with your
agency on the movie-going habits of the California Legislature.

Please try to get back to me within the next week or two on the
subject of the identification cards.

My best regards.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        ALAN ROBBINS

sections.question_synthetic: What disclosure and valuation requirements apply to Motion Picture Council passes provided to state legislators, and how should these gifts be reported under California's conflict of interest laws?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC clarifies that the estimated value of Motion Picture Council passes must be based on the estimated long-term use rather than average use, and participants must disclose the cards with appropriate notation directing inquiries to the FPPC or Motion Picture Council for details.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is severely degraded with significant OCR errors and text fragmentation. No clear QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Extracted information based on partial reconstruction of content discussing Motion Picture Council card valuation and reporting requirements. Date appears to be June 16, 1980 (FPPC response) referencing April 30, 1980 (earlier correspondence). High uncertainty due to document quality.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What disclosure and valuation requirements apply to Motion Picture Council passes provided to state legislators, and how should these gifts be reported under California's conflict of interest laws?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC clarifies that the estimated value of Motion Picture Council passes must be based on the estimated long-term use rather than average use, and participants must disclose the cards with appropriate notation directing inquiries to the FPPC or Motion Picture Council for details.

embedding.summary: This is FPPC correspondence responding to Senator Alan Robbins regarding the proper disclosure and valuation of passes provided by the Motion Picture Council to state legislators under California conflict of interest law.
